title: Carved hazel preserve spoon
subtitle: Green hazel jam and preserve spoon for the kitchen shelf
excerpt: A preserve spoon carved from green hazel: short-handled, wide-bowled, and deep enough to scoop a thick set jam cleanly. Hazel is a reliable coppice wood that carves well green and dries without splitting if the walls are kept even. A good beginner spoon after the mustard spoon.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for general treen; hazel as a coppice-source carving wood is documented in early-twentieth-century basketry and craft manuals including Newey and Drage.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools before it dries.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, cutting off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A curved-bladed knife for hollowing the bowl of a spoon or similar vessel.

content.text: Hazel is the coppice-woodland staple: fast-growing, widespread, and reliable to carve from a fresh rod or split billet. A preserve spoon sits in a jam jar or chutney pot all day; it wants a deep bowl that holds a spoonful cleanly, a short handle that clears the jar lip, and walls even enough that the wood dries without cracking. This is a step up from the mustard spoon and a natural second project for a beginner: the
blank
is slightly larger and the bowl slightly deeper, which means more
hook-knife
work and more attention to grain direction.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
A
green

hazel billet: 18 cm long, 6 cm across the flat face, 3.5 cm deep. Hazel coppices freely; a freshly cut rod 6 to 8 cm in diameter gives this sized billet when split in two. Community woodlands, garden centres with coppiced stock, and tree surgeons are all reasonable sources. Birch and sycamore are good alternatives if hazel is not available fresh.
Tools
A sloyd knife for the handle and profile, a medium hook knife for the bowl, a leather strop, and raw linseed oil. The preserve spoon is small enough that an axe is not needed for shaping.
Method
Draw the profile on the flat face: handle 11 cm, neck 1.5 cm, bowl oval 4 cm long and 3.5 cm wide. Mark the bowl depth on the side face at 1.5 cm below the flat face.
Remove waste with
push cuts
on the sloyd knife: blade away from the body, four-finger grip on the blank. Work down to the pencil line in several passes rather than trying to reach it in one heavy cut.
Shape the handle to an oval cross-section. The back of the handle should be slightly rounded; the face slightly flatter; to sit naturally in the hand when scooping from a deep jar.

Hollow the bowl with the medium hook knife, working from the rim toward the centre with the grain. The bowl should reach 1.5 cm depth at the lowest point. Check by pushing a pencil into the bowl and marking where it meets the rim; that mark should show at 1.5 cm.
Refine the outside of the bowl with push cuts. The outside curve should match the inside hollow: evenly rounded, not flat-backed. Walls 3 to 4 mm throughout.

Leave to dry 2 weeks. Hazel dries quickly; if you hear or see a crack appearing in the bowl, it means one wall is thicker than the other. At this stage, carve the thicker wall thinner while the wood is still damp enough to cut.
Finishing
Sand with 120 and 180 grit. Apply raw linseed oil liberally; leave an hour; wipe off the surplus. Two-week cure before food contact. Re-oil monthly in regular use.
Variations
Chutney spoon: a narrower oval bowl to fit a standard chutney jar; same handle length.
Honey spoon variant: a round bowl replacing the oval; the round form pours honey from the spoon rather than holding it.
Left-handed: invert the neck angle so the bowl sits at a natural tilt for a left-handed scoop from a right-opening jar lid.
Care

Wash with warm water after each use. Towel-dry at once. Never leave standing in jam or pickle for long periods; the acid and sugar content will dull the linseed finish over time. Re-oil every few months.

items.symptom: Bowl too shallow after hook-knife work
items.cause: Hook knife angled too flat, taking thin shavings from the rim rather than the centre
items.fix: Angle the hook knife more steeply toward the centre of the bowl on each stroke. The deepest point should be carved before the rim thickness is finalised.

items.symptom: Hairline crack appears in the bowl during drying
items.cause: One wall thicker than the other; uneven drying stress
items.fix: Carve the thicker wall thinner while the wood is still slightly damp. A hairline crack that opens during drying usually stays minor if the walls are evened; a crack that widens to a visible gap means the bowl is lost.
